reason: Specific ask tied to a concrete root cause and a concrete spec rule: "shared string formats (cache keys, event names) live in exactly one constant".
Correctly diagnoses a shallow pass-through module and forces a real decision: "Either give the controller a real responsibility ... or delete it".
Correctly names temporal decomposition ("four passes, each touching every record") and gives a measurable, actionable fix and metric.
Correct interface critique with a concrete target: "Target signature: parseConfig(path, opts={})", tied to real per-format cost.
Sound application of design-it-twice, forcing a real alternative ("require one written alternative: a server-push (SSE/webhook) design") with a decision record.
Concrete, checkable renames ("data2 -> pendingRetries; handle -> openSocket") gated on reviewer sign-off; correctly ties vague names to obscurity/cognitive load.
Correctly identifies a special-general mixture smeared across five methods and gives an actionable, principled fix ("resolve VIP to a rate table once at the entry point").
Pure generic PM boilerplate with no design specifics: "Ensure the team follows engineering best practices and writes clean code."
Vague with no specifics: "Consider refactoring the areas that feel over-engineered" names no module, no interface, no evidence.

Confidently wrong: mandates splitting a 900-line module into 6-7 pieces "so each does one thing" and a mechanical "break up any class over 300 lines" rule -- exactly the size-driven shallow-decomposition Ousterhout warns against (smaller modules add interfaces without necessarily reducing complexity).

Confidently wrong technical reasoning: dismisses a duplicated cache-key literal as safe because "caches are eventually consistent," which doesn't address why two different literals would ever collide on the same key.
Generic launch-checklist boilerplate ("Update the runbook and schedule the launch review") with zero connection to any design/complexity issue.

Misapplies the principle: "add more comments and documentation everywhere" and a docstring-on-every-function mandate papers over bad design with comments rather than fixing complexity, contrary to Ousterhout's guidance that comments shouldn't compensate for poor structure.

Adds an indirection layer whose only justification is "adds a clean seam and follows separation-of-concerns" -- no stated hidden complexity, i.e. a likely shallow pass-through adapter, the exact anti-pattern flagged elsewhere in this set (L02).

Correctly isolates the real lever ("make the event name a parameter so adding an event is a call, not a new method"); the boilerplate tail doesn't undo the specific, correct, actionable core recommendation.

Confidently wrong: splitting AuthService into four services "ahead of the features that will need them" is speculative generality -- designing for anticipated future needs rather than a present one, which Ousterhout treats as a complexity trap, not a win.
Two specific, correct, actionable fixes: push format flags out of parseConfig's signature and make the cache key a single named constant.

Names the right concept but stays vague with no concrete path: "Consider deepening it or revisiting the abstraction" and "Encourage the team to think about information hiding" give a PM nothing specific to ask or check.
